basic_data.social_status: Naib under Shamil — high-ranking military-religious commander; famous warrior; currently fugitive reduced to one murid
basic_data.physical_signature: Prominent cheekbones and a slight limp when walking — the limp betrays every covert movement; the cheekbones catch torchlight in interiors and are unmistakable even when his face is wrapped in the hood of his burka. For casting: the actor must carry a measured, weight-shifted gait that never fully hides the injury, paired with a still, hawk-like face that moves only at the eyes.

psychology.want: To reach the Russians alive, secure their protection, and use their power to free his family from Shamil and return to take revenge — a concrete, operational goal driving every scene.
psychology.need: To recognize that no sovereign — neither Shamil nor the Tsar — will treat him as a man rather than an instrument; his need is the acceptance of sovereign solitude, the warrior's dignity that does not depend on a patron.
psychology.fear: That his family (mother, wives, sons) will be seized or killed by Shamil before he can act — and beneath that, the fear of dying as a pawn in another man's war.
psychology.contradiction: A legendary warrior who travels disguised and silent; a man of Islamic devotion who prepares to ally with Christian occupiers; a commander who commands murids yet defers with reverence to an old apiarist and accepts indirect answers without pressing.

arc.act_1_start: Famous naib reduced to fugitive — arrives at night in a hostile aoul with one murid, still carrying the posture and authority of command but stripped of banner and escort. Believes strategy and alliance can save his family.
arc.act_2_change: Enters Russian orbit — courted, watched, measured. Learns that the Russian court treats him as a trophy, not an ally; that Vorontsov's courtesy and Nikolaus's indifference are two faces of the same empire. Family remains hostage; every diplomatic path narrows.
arc.act_3_end: Breaks out in a final ride — chooses death in motion over captivity in either court. Dies outnumbered, severed from both worlds, having refused to be anyone's instrument. Head paraded; legend intact; man erased.

voice_profile.voice_description: Hadji Murad speaks in short, functional sentences — every word is an action or a probe, never ornament. His register moves between the ritualized formality of Islamic and Chechen greeting (elevated, cadenced, near-liturgical) and the clipped imperative of a commander assigning a task. Silence is his primary rhetorical tool: he listens with his eyes on the door, and when he speaks it is to decide.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal-plain. Educated in religious and military tradition, not in courtly rhetoric. Uses Chechen and Arabic-Islamic formulas (Selaam aleikum, Ne habar, Habar yok) in greetings and questions. Archaic second person in English rendering (thou, thy, canst) for gravitas; no abstract, sentimental, or introspective vocabulary.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 5–10 words. Predominantly declarative and imperative. Questions are direct and singular (never compound). Fragments appear under pressure. Almost never uses subordinate clauses — thought moves by juxtaposition, not by hinging.
verbal_tics: Opens exchanges with ritual greeting before any content ('Selaam aleikum', 'May thy sons live')
Asks 'Ne habar?' (Is there anything new?) as standing probe for intelligence
Introduces subordinates by function and name in one beat ('My murid. Eldar is his name.')
'Now we must —' as pivot from listening to decision
Nods once instead of acknowledging verbally
avoidances: Never explains his feelings — no 'I am afraid', 'I am tired', 'I miss'
Never complains about Shamil, the Russians, or fate — grievance is beneath a naib
Never uses abstract or philosophical language ('freedom', 'destiny', 'honor' as concepts) — honor is shown in conduct, not named
Never begs, pleads, or negotiates in emotional register — requests are framed as assignments or questions
Never interrupts an elder; never presses a host who has signaled reticence
Never swears, never jokes loosely, never uses diminutives or endearments even with his murid
Never speaks of his family by name in front of strangers — they exist as silence
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feeling is carried almost entirely in body and tempo: the slight limp, a hand closing on the hilt of the sword, closed eyes during prayer, listening past the speaker to sounds outside the door. Verbal emotion appears only as compression — a shortened sentence, a pivot mid-exchange, a name spoken once. Love and grief for his family surface only in ritual phrasing ('May thy sons live') displaced onto others.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured with sudden accelerations. Long stillnesses of listening, then a cluster of imperative sentences that resolve a decision. Never hectic, never meandering.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_Ne_habar_he_means: Is Shamil's hand already on my family, and is this house still safe for me tonight?
subtext_rules.when_he_nods_slightly_he_means: I have heard what you refused to say, and I will not force you to say it.
subtext_rules.when_he_greets_formally_he_means: I am placing myself under the protection of your hearth; betrayal of me is now betrayal of your house.
subtext_rules.when_he_gives_an_order_without_preamble_: The situation is worse than I will tell you; act now so I do not have to explain why.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_mid_conversation_he: I am listening for horses; continue talking so the room sounds normal.
subtext_rules.when_he_speaks_of_the_Russians_as_a_dest: I have no allies left; I am choosing the lesser captor.

example_dialog_lines.under_pressure: Now we must send a man with a letter to the Russians. My murid will go. He will need a guide.
example_dialog_lines.hopeful_joyful: May thy sons live, and thy house stand after thee.
example_dialog_lines.angry_hurt: Shamil has my mother. He has my wives. He has my sons. I do not need to hear more.
example_dialog_lines.vulnerable_honest: I have ridden with a banner and fifty murids. Tonight I ride with one. A man learns what he is when the banner is down.

relationships.quality: Subordinate-in-rebellion. Reverence for the Imamate collides with the certainty that Shamil will kill his family to break him.
relationships.power_dynamic: Shamil holds the hostages; Hadji Murad holds his own legend and his sword. Asymmetric — Shamil can act, Hadji Murad can only move.
relationships.register_shift: Never names Shamil casually; when forced to speak of him, uses the title, not the man. Tone flattens to pure operational language.

relationships.quality: Bond of function and faith; Eldar is introduced with a single sentence and positioned with a glance.
relationships.power_dynamic: Absolute authority, absolutely trusted; Eldar obeys without verbal order.
relationships.register_shift: Shortest possible sentences, imperatives, no softening.

relationships.quality: Mutual obligation: Sado shelters at mortal risk; Hadji Murad honors Sado's counsel by listening fully and acting on it.
relationships.power_dynamic: Equal — host and warrior-guest; sacred parity under the law of kunachestvo.
relationships.register_shift: Ritualized greeting, attentive silence, approving nod; direct but never commanding.

relationships.type: younger warrior to elder of the village
relationships.quality: Deferential: performs prayer beside him, accepts evasive answers, does not press.
relationships.power_dynamic: Elder holds moral authority; Hadji Murad holds rank but bows his posture.
relationships.register_shift: Elevated, liturgical, patient; questions singular and soft.

relationships.quality: Strategic alliance-in-offering; mutual use dressed as mutual respect.
relationships.power_dynamic: Vorontsov holds the empire's machinery; Hadji Murad holds intelligence and legend. Courteous parity on the surface, captivity underneath.
relationships.register_shift: Most formal register, ritual compliments translated through an interpreter, no volunteered information.

relationships.quality: Purely functional; assessed in one question.
relationships.register_shift: Immediate task-framing, no preamble: 'Canst thou conduct my murid to the Russians?'

relationships.type: guest to hostess under Islamic etiquette
relationships.quality: Formal, minimal, reciprocal; she is a presence of the hearth, not an interlocutor.
relationships.power_dynamic: Ritualized — her hospitality binds him; his greeting honors the house.
relationships.register_shift: Single ceremonial line, eyes averted, no further address.

relationships.type: hostages; the absent center of every action
relationships.quality: Unspoken. They drive every decision and appear in dialogue only as silence or as displaced blessings onto other men's sons.
relationships.power_dynamic: They are the gravity; he is the orbit.
relationships.register_shift: He never names them aloud in company. When alone or broken, their names are the only words that would come.
